specification.topic-statement: The tool discovery system scans configurable directories for tool executables and populates the tool registry with their definitions.

scope.in-scope: Directory scanning, executable detection, schema extraction via subprocess invocation, tool name derivation, and registration with override precedence.
scope.boundary: This topic sends tool names, paths, and parsed schema documents to the registry. How the registry stores or retrieves entries is outside this topic's scope.
This topic parses the raw JSON schema output from tools but does not validate its structure or interpret its contents. The schema is passed opaquely to the registry.
This topic reads directory listings and file metadata from the file system. Directory creation, modification, and any writes are outside scope.

field.name: name
field.note: derived from filename by stripping -tool suffix and converting hyphens to underscores

field.name: path
field.note: full filesystem path to the executable

field.name: schema
field.note: output of running the executable with --schema

behavior.scan-order-and-override-precedence:
Discovery scans three directories in a fixed order: system, then user, then project. When a tool with the same name appears in more than one directory, the later-scanned directory's version replaces the earlier one. Project tools override user tools; user tools override system tools.
If any directory scan returns an error, discovery stops immediately and propagates the error without scanning subsequent directories.
After all three directories have been scanned, the registry is sorted alphabetically by tool name.
behavior.scanning-a-directory:
Each directory is opened for reading. If the directory does not exist or cannot be opened, it is silently skipped — no error is produced and discovery continues with the next directory.
A missing or inaccessible directory is not an error condition. Discovery simply moves on. This allows a system with no user-level tools, or no project-level tools, to operate normally.
Every entry in the directory is examined in the order returned by the operating system. The entries named "." and ".." are skipped. For each remaining entry, the full file path is constructed by joining the directory path and the entry name.
An entry is eligible for registration only if both conditions are met: 1. The file has the user-execute permission bit set (determined by inspecting the file's metadata; if metadata cannot be read, the file is skipped silently). 2. The filename ends with the suffix "-tool".
Files that fail either condition are silently skipped without error.
behavior.schema-extraction:
For each eligible file, a child process is spawned to run the tool executable with the single argument "--schema". The child process's standard output is captured. Standard error is discarded silently.
The parent waits for the child's output with a one-second timeout enforced via a system alarm signal. If the child does not produce output or does not exit within one second, the alarm fires.
The child's output is read into a buffer with a maximum capacity of 8191 bytes (plus a null terminator for 8192 bytes total). If the tool produces more output than this, the excess is silently truncated.
After reading completes, the alarm is cancelled, the pipe is closed, and the parent waits for the child process to exit.
A schema extraction is considered successful only if both of these are true: - The child process exited normally with exit status zero. - At least one byte was read from its output.
If either condition fails, the tool is silently skipped — no error is produced and discovery continues with the next file.
A tool that crashes, produces no output, or exits with a non-zero status is ignored without any warning or log message. The tool simply does not appear in the registry.
If the output is non-empty and the child exited successfully, the output is parsed as JSON. If JSON parsing fails, the tool is silently skipped.
behavior.tool-name-derivation:
A tool's registry name is derived from its filename by two transformations: 1. The trailing "-tool" suffix is removed. 2. Every hyphen in the remaining name is replaced with an underscore.
Examples: - "bash-tool" becomes "bash" - "file-read-tool" becomes "file_read" - "git-commit-tool" becomes "git_commit"
If the file does not end with "-tool", the behavior is undefined — this case is treated as a precondition violation.
behavior.registration:
Each successfully extracted tool is added to the registry by name, path, and schema. If a tool with the same name already exists in the registry (from an earlier directory scan), the existing entry is replaced with the new one.
If registration fails (due to a registry-internal error), the error is propagated immediately and discovery halts.
